title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Netherlands — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Espresso is small and strong
items.text: Dutch espresso ('espresso' or 'koffie verkeerd' if you want it milky) arrives in a tiny cup—usually just 1-2 ounces. Don't expect American-sized portions; this is authentic European coffee culture.

items.label: Coffee comes with stroopwafels and biscuits
items.text: Most cafés automatically serve a small cookie or stroopwafel with your coffee. It's included and part of the experience—eating it while the coffee is hot is the proper way.

items.label: Café versus Kroeg
items.text: A 'café' is a daytime coffee/lunch spot; a 'kroeg' is a pub or bar serving alcohol. Many places blur this line, but the distinction matters for atmosphere and opening hours.

items.label: Try a brown café (bruine kroeg)
items.text: These traditional, wood-paneled neighborhood pubs serve coffee by day and beer by night. They're authentic, cozy, and free of tourist polish—Amsterdam's Café de Dokter is a classic example.

items.label: Bitterballen
items.text: Deep-fried meat croquettes about the size of a golf ball, filled with ragout. Served warm with mustard, they're the quintessential Dutch bar snack—crispy outside, creamy inside.

items.label: Poffertjes
items.text: Tiny, puffy pancakes dusted with powdered sugar and topped with a small pat of butter. Sold from street stalls, these are sweet, warm, and genuinely addictive—not a full meal but a perfect snack.

items.label: Haring (raw herring)
items.text: Fresh, lightly salted herring served whole with onions and gherkins, traditionally held by the tail and eaten in one bite. It's an acquired taste, but a crucial Dutch food experience—buy from a proper fishmonger, not tourist stands.

items.label: Kroket
items.text: A larger cousin of the bitterbal, this fried croquette contains ragout and is often filled with meat or vegetables. Served hot with mustard, it's hearty street food or a light meal.

items.label: Stamppot
items.text: A rustic mash of potatoes combined with vegetables (like kale, carrots, or spinach) and topped with sausage or bacon. It's comfort food—hearty, warming, and genuinely Dutch home cooking.

items.label: Pannenkoeken
items.text: Dutch pancakes that are thinner and more savory than American pancakes, often topped with bacon, cheese, or jam. They're available for breakfast, lunch, or dinner at dedicated pancake restaurants.

items.label: Bread is rarely free
items.text: Unlike many European countries, you'll typically be charged for bread in restaurants—usually €1–3 per basket. It's automatically placed but optional; let servers know if you don't want it.

items.label: Portion sizes are smaller than North America
items.text: Dutch portions are modest by international standards. If you're very hungry, order an appetizer and main, or ask for extra vegetables; locals often eat light lunches and substantial dinners.

items.label: Specials (dagschotel) offer value
items.text: Most restaurants display a daily special (often on a blackboard), which is cheaper than the à la carte menu and highlights fresh, seasonal ingredients. It's a reliable way to eat well on budget.

items.label: English menus are standard in tourist areas, risky elsewhere
items.text: In Amsterdam, The Hague, and Rotterdam, English menus are common. In smaller towns or traditional establishments, menus may be Dutch-only—don't hesitate to ask for English or use a translation app.

items.label: Water is not free
items.text: Tap water is not served automatically; you must order and pay for bottled water (€2–4). If you want still water, ask for 'kraanwater,' which many establishments will provide free, though it's sometimes served reluctantly.

items.label: Street food stands (snackbars)
items.text: Casual counters selling fries, croquettes, and fried foods—often the cheapest and most authentic option. Look for locals queuing; the best ones are packed at lunch and after nights out.

items.label: Grand Café and Eetcafé
items.text: These casual, all-day establishments serve breakfast, lunch, and dinner in a relaxed atmosphere—good for families and groups. They're less formal than restaurants but more refined than snackbars.

items.label: Avoid tourist-trap restaurants near major attractions
items.text: Restaurants directly next to Anne Frank House, Dam Square, or train stations typically overcharge for mediocre food. Walk 5–10 minutes away to find better value and authentic local spots.

items.label: Pannenkoeken restaurants
items.text: Dedicated spots serving sweet and savory Dutch pancakes—casual, informal, and affordable. Perfect for a light meal any time of day; they're family-friendly and reliably good.

items.label: Reservation tips
items.text: High-end and popular mid-range restaurants require reservations, especially Thursday–Saturday. Call ahead or use TheFork or Google; walk-ins may wait 45+ minutes or be turned away during peak hours.

items.label: Beer (Pilsner style)
items.text: Dutch beer is typically a crisp, golden pilsner—Heineken and Amstel are global brands, but local breweries like Brouwerij 't IJ and Oost are far superior. Beer is served cold in small glasses; order 'een pint' for the standard 250ml serving.

items.label: Jenever (Dutch gin)
items.text: A juniper-based spirit stronger and more complex than London gin, often served as a chaser with beer or as a shot. Traditionally consumed in one gulp from a small glass; it's a cultural ritual and genuinely warming.

items.label: Wine is expensive and not the national pride
items.text: The Netherlands doesn't produce significant wine. Restaurant markups are steep; wine is better suited for casual drinking at home than fine dining. Beer and jenever are far better value.

items.label: Advocaat
items.text: A thick, creamy liqueur made from eggs and brandy—rich and sweet, often drunk as a digestif or mixed into coffee. It's distinctly Dutch and an acquired taste but worth trying.

items.label: Soft drinks cost nearly as much as beer
items.text: Expect to pay €2–3 for a small soft drink, similar to beer prices. This is normal; water is the cheapest option if you ask for 'kraanwater.'

items.label: Dutch are direct about time and reservations
items.text: Arrive on time for reservations or cancel in advance. Tables may be turned quickly, especially at lunch; lingering for hours is considered inconsiderate. If you're running late, call the restaurant.

items.label: Service is not intrusive; flag down servers when needed
items.text: Dutch servers don't hover and won't check on you constantly. Make eye contact, raise your hand, or say 'Juffrouw!' (for a woman) or 'Meneer!' (for a man) to request service. This is normal and expected, not rude.

items.label: Tipping is modest but appreciated
items.text: Service charges are included in most bills. Rounding up to the nearest €5 or leaving 5–10% for good service is standard; 15% is generous. Tipping is never required but is polite.

items.label: Bills are typically one check per table
items.text: The waiter won't automatically split the bill. Ask politely ('Kunnen we apart betalen?') in advance, or settle it amongst yourselves afterward. Splitting bills per person is less common than in other countries.

items.label: Meals follow a light breakfast, modest lunch, substantial dinner pattern
items.text: Breakfast (ontbijt) is light—bread, cheese, jam. Lunch (lunch) is a sandwich or small meal around noon. Dinner (avondeten) is the main meal, typically 6–8 PM. Plan your eating accordingly.

items.label: Respect smoking and bike parking culture
items.text: Smoking indoors is banned in restaurants, but outdoor terraces may allow it. Don't park bikes in restricted areas near eating establishments, and be aware of your surroundings—these are serious local conventions.

items.label: Kroketten and Bitterballen (winter)
items.text: These fried snacks are especially popular October–March when the weather is cold. They're warming, savory, and pair perfectly with winter beers.

items.label: Asparagus (spring, April–June)
items.text: White asparagus is a seasonal delicacy, served with melted butter, ham, and boiled eggs. It appears on spring menus and is celebrated; quality asparagus is a sign of a good kitchen.

items.label: Cheese (year-round but celebrated May)
items.text: Dutch cheese (Gouda, Edam, Maasdam) is available always, but May is 'Cheese Month' with special events and cheese markets. Try aged varieties (18+ months) for deeper flavor; avoid tourist-trap cheese shops in Amsterdam.
